---
title: "Panel Conditioning and Mortality"
subtitle: "Evidence from UK Birth Cohort Studies"
author: "Jason Fletcher"
date: today
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    toc: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Motivation

- Long-run cohort studies are a cornerstone of social and health science research.
- But **does being surveyed change you?** — *panel conditioning*
- If so: cohort participants ≠ a random sample; conclusions may be confounded.

**Key question:** Do individuals born in weeks *selected* for UK panel studies
have **different mortality** than similar individuals born in nearby, unselected weeks?

---

## The UK natural experiment

Three major UK studies each selected **a single birth week**:

| Study | Birth week | N (approx.) |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| NSHD | 3–9 March **1946** | ~5,000 followed |
| NCDS | 3–9 March **1958** | ~17,000 followed |
| BCS70 | 5–11 April **1970** | ~17,000 followed |

Adjacent weeks provide a natural **control group** — same cohort, same season, never surveyed.

---

## Data

- **ONS administrative vital statistics**: deaths by week of birth × calendar year,
  England and Wales, 1970–2013.
- Covers all deaths registered; not self-reported.
- Unit of analysis: birth week × calendar year (mortality rate).
- Three cohort groups × 9 weeks (1 treated + 8 control) each.

---

## Empirical strategy

$$\text{rate}_{b,t} = \alpha + \beta \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{treated week}]_b
  + \gamma_a + \delta_t + \eta_w + \varepsilon_{b,t}$$

- $\beta$ = panel conditioning effect on mortality rate
- Controls: **age FE**, **calendar year FE**, **week-of-year FE**
- Standard errors clustered by birth week
- Also estimated by **10-year age intervals**

---

## Main result — pooled

[INSERT: TABLE/FIGURE with pooled $\hat\beta$ and 95% CI from `output/tables/`]

---

## By age interval

[INSERT: FIGURE — $\hat\beta$ by age bin (0–9, 10–19, ..., 60–69) with 95% CI]

---

## Robustness

[FILL IN after analysis]

---

## Comparison: Wisconsin Longitudinal Study

@Warren2022_wls_mortality use a true experimental design (WLS):

- 1/3 of 1957 Wisconsin HS seniors randomly selected to participate
- **50+ years** of intensive survey follow-up
- Result: **no effect on longevity** (full sample + subgroups)

Our UK evidence: [FILL IN — complement or contrast]

---

## Conclusion

- [FILL IN after results]
- Denominator note: treated and control at-risk populations are balanced by design
  (same birth calendar cluster, same geography).

---

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
